input_path: data\papers\Zech et al. - Model-driven Digital Twins for AECO.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study:
The **Physical Twin system** under study is a **BIM-based infrastructure project** within the **AECO (Architecture, Engineering, Construction, and Operations) domain**, focusing on **building life-cycle management**. The system comprises **semantically rich, object-based 3D models** generated via BIM, incorporating **geometric, spatial, and functional characteristics** of building components (e.g., structural elements, MEP systems, façade assemblies) and their **interrelationships** (e.g., spatial adjacency, performance dependencies). Key characteristics include:
- **Policy-driven adoption**: Mandated by regulatory initiatives and industry standards (e.g., [60]) to ensure standardized digital representation.
- **Collaborative framework**: Enables multi-disciplinary coordination (architects, engineers, construction professionals) for **clash detection**, **performance simulation**, and **documentation generation**.
- **Lifecycle-centric**: Supports **design-to-operations continuity**, bridging gaps between **design-time models** (e.g., IFC/BIM formats) and **runtime operational data** (e.g., sensor feeds, maintenance logs).
- **Asset granularity**: Models individual components (e.g., HVAC units, doors, structural beams) with **functional attributes** (e.g., thermal properties, load-bearing capacity) and **temporal dynamics** (e.g., degradation over time).
- **Interoperability**: Aligns with **industry standards** (e.g., Industry Foundation Classes, IFC) for seamless data exchange across AECO workflows.

extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The Digital Twin system enforces **real-time data exchange capabilities** to ensure continuous synchronization between physical building assets and their digital counterparts, eliminating temporal discrepancies between static BIM representations and dynamic operational states. The framework mandates **live synchronization** to support immersive monitoring and control interfaces, though explicit temporal granularities (e.g., millisecond-level control actions or second-to-minute-level analytics) are not specified. Synchronization frequencies are implied to be **continuous** (without defined intervals) to meet the AECO domain’s operational demands, with runtime configuration management enabling dynamic adjustments to latency requirements without full redeployment. The system bridges static BIM models with dynamic operational needs, emphasizing **temporal alignment** via bidirectional data flows but omitting precise latency thresholds or service-level time scales.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The Digital Twin framework adopts a **modular, hierarchical architecture** with **multiple digital twin instances** deployed across infrastructure systems, supporting **scalability from individual components to entire building portfolios**. The system distinguishes roles: **Building Control Engineers** manage **device-level twins** (e.g., HVAC, lighting), while **Facility Managers** interact with **facility-wide twins** for holistic monitoring. **DT Engineers** extend capabilities via **custom service integration**, enabling **decentralized yet coordinated twin instances** with well-defined scopes. Coordination mechanisms rely on **centralized runtime management** (via the DT Framework as a hub) to ensure **bidirectional data exchange** and **consistent state synchronization** across instances, though explicit details on decentralization protocols (e.g., blockchain, peer-to-peer) are not provided.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The Digital Twin system follows a **centralized orchestration pattern** with a **DT Framework acting as the core hub**, coordinating roles (Building Control Engineers, Facility Managers, DT Engineers) and system components. The architecture leverages **modular extension points** for **service integration**, enabling dynamic addition of analytics or control modules without disrupting core functionality. **Resource management** is implicit in the framework’s design, prioritizing **low-overhead updates** and **maintainability** through runtime configuration management. System-wide coordination relies on **standardized interfaces** and **bidirectional data flows** to bridge enriched BIM models (static) with operational DTs (dynamic), ensuring end-to-end life cycle management. No explicit mention of **microservices, event-driven architectures, or federated DT constellations** is provided.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: The Digital Twin system prioritizes **interoperability across heterogeneous systems** through **standardized extension points** and a **requirements-driven approach**, ensuring seamless integration with external tools and platforms. While specific protocols (e.g., OPC UA, BACnet) or standards (e.g., Industry Foundation Classes) are not named, the framework leverages **Eclipse Hono and Ditto** for data exchange, enabling bidirectional communication with third-party systems. **Dependencies on external systems** are implied but not detailed, with a focus on **low-overhead updates** to maintain compatibility. The system does not explicitly reference ERP, CMMS, or other enterprise systems, instead emphasizing **modularity** and **scalability** to accommodate future integrations via the central hub’s standardized interfaces.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data:
The Digital Twin (DT) system employs a **model-driven deployment framework** that integrates **Building Information Modeling (BIM)** as the foundational data source, augmented with **Model-Driven Engineering (MDE)** principles. The computational models include:
- **Geometric Models**: Derived from BIM, representing building structures and spatial configurations.
- **Behavioral Models**: Defined by **Building Control Engineers** to encapsulate **sensors, actuators, and controllers** (BCS components), enabling dynamic control logic within the BIM environment. These models are semantically enriched to bridge BIM’s static limitations with DT’s dynamic requirements.
- **Analytical Models**: Generated via MDE transformations to produce **deployable DT artifacts** (e.g., simulation models, control algorithms) from structured BIM representations.

**Data Sources**:
- Primary: BIM models (static geometric/structural data).
- Secondary: Real-time data streams from **sensors and actuators** (dynamic operational data).
- Custom: Extensible via **DT Engineer-developed services/plugins** (e.g., IoT sensor feeds, third-party analytics).

**Data Management Approaches**:
- **Unified Semantic Foundation**: Enables interoperability between BIM and DT components through standardized ontologies.
- **Model-Driven Transformation**: Automates extraction of structured representations (e.g., JSON/XML schemas) from enriched BIM models.
- **Modular Architecture**: Supports **standardized extension points** for integrating custom services, ensuring scalability and adaptability.
- **Real-Time Synchronization**: Facilitates continuous bidirectional data exchange between physical infrastructure and the DT via **interactive visualization interfaces**.

**Model Relationships**:
- **Hierarchical**: BIM models (static) → Enriched BIM (semantic) → Deployable DT artifacts (dynamic).
- **Dependency-Based**: Behavioral models depend on geometric models for spatial context; control logic relies on sensor/actuator data for real-time adjustments.
- **Service-Oriented**: Custom plugins/services interact with core DT models via **modular interfaces**, enabling plug-and-play functionality.

**Roles in Data Constellation**:
- **Building Control Engineers** define and configure device models (sensors/actuators/controllers) within BIM.
- **Facility Managers** interact with the DT for monitoring/control via real-time data visualizations.
- **DT Engineers** extend functionality by integrating custom services (e.g., predictive analytics, energy optimization) into the modular framework.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components:
The framework defines **physical actuators and control mechanisms** as **embedded components within BIM models**, configured by **Building Control Engineers** to implement control logic for Building Control Systems (BCS). These actuators are **remotely operable** by **Facility Managers** via the Digital Twin interface, enabling **real-time control of physical infrastructure** (e.g., HVAC systems, lighting, shading).

**Key characteristics include**:
- **Control Capabilities**: Actuators are modeled to interact with **sensors and controllers** defined in the BIM environment, enabling closed-loop system operation.
- **Remote Control Interfaces**: Facility Managers interact with actuators through the Digital Twin’s **interactive visualization layer**, though specific protocols (e.g., KNX, BACnet) are **not mentioned in the documents**.
- **Safety Constraints**: While not explicitly detailed, the framework implies **safety mechanisms** are inherent to the **model-driven deployment process**, ensuring alignment between logical and physical system states.
- **Actuation Ranges/Limitations**: Defined implicitly via **BIM-embedded constraints** (e.g., physical boundaries, operational limits) during the modeling phase by Building Control Engineers.
- **Addressing Mechanisms**: Actuators are addressed via **semantic identifiers** derived from the BIM model, ensuring traceability and integration with the Digital Twin’s runtime environment.

**No explicit details on fail-safes, precision ranges, or actuation frequencies are provided in the documents.**
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components:
The Digital Twin system incorporates **sensing infrastructure** as part of the BIM-enriched models, though specific sensor types (e.g., PT100, NDIR), hardware platforms, or communication protocols are not detailed. Key aspects include:
- **Sensor Types**:
  - Implicitly categorized as **sensors** within BIM models, enabling data collection for **post-occupancy analytics** (referenced in TwinLight/BOREALIS projects).
- **Measurement Capabilities**:
  - Not specified; assumed to align with BIM’s general-purpose modeling (e.g., temperature, occupancy, energy usage) but lacks technical precision (e.g., ±0.5°C accuracy).
- **Spatial Distribution**:
  - Defined within BIM models as part of **device modeling**, with spatial relationships mapped to physical infrastructure for synchronization.
- **Sampling Frequencies**:
  - Not documented; inferred to be configurable via runtime data exchange mechanisms but not quantified.
- **Data Transmission Rates**:
  - Implicitly supported by **real-time data exchange** capabilities for continuous synchronization, though bandwidth or latency metrics are absent.
- **Accuracy and Precision Specifications**:
  - Not provided; constrained by BIM’s limitations and the framework’s reliance on **model-driven validation** (referenced in fidelity considerations).
- **Data Collection Mechanisms**:
  - **BIM-integrated sensor definitions** serve as the primary data source, with runtime artifacts handling **real-time aggregation** for analytics.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations:
The framework addresses **model fidelity and validity** through a **multi-layered approach**, primarily centered on **Overall Digital Twin Entanglement (ODTE)**—a metric referenced in the abstract to quantify the alignment between the Digital Twin and physical system. **Validation and verification** are implicitly supported via:

1. **Model Fidelity Requirements**:
   - **Semantic Consistency**: Enforced via **MDE transformations** that validate the **unified semantic foundation** between BIM models and Digital Twin artifacts.
   - **Dynamic Synchronization**: Real-time data exchange ensures **continuity of state** between the physical and digital twins, mitigating divergence.

2. **Validation and Verification Methods**:
   - **Automated Semantic Checks**: During MDE transformations, the framework verifies that **sensors, actuators, and controllers** are correctly mapped from BIM to deployable artifacts.
   - **Runtime Integrity Checks**: **Not explicitly detailed**; implied to occur via **real-time data synchronization** mechanisms (e.g., Eclipse Hono/Ditto) to detect discrepancies.

3. **Uncertainty Sources and Quantification**:
   - **ODTE Metric**: Quantifies **entanglement** (e.g., synchronization latency, data accuracy) but lacks **specific algorithms or thresholds** for uncertainty quantification.
   - **BIM Limitations**: Addressed by **augmenting static BIM models** with dynamic BCS components, reducing but not eliminating uncertainty in geometric-behavioral mappings.

4. **Error Detection and Correction Mechanisms**:
   - **Not explicitly described**; inferred to rely on **automated MDE validation** and **runtime synchronization** to flag inconsistencies (e.g., missing sensor data, control logic mismatches).
   - **Quality Assurance Processes**: Implicit in the **model-driven lifecycle**, where **DT Engineers** and **Building Control Engineers** iteratively refine models to ensure fidelity.

**No explicit mention of error correction algorithms, automated retraining, or human-in-the-loop validation mechanisms.**
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: The **physical-to-virtual interaction** in this Digital Twin (DT) framework is enabled through **real-time data exchange** facilitated by **Eclipse Hono** and **Ditto**, which integrate live operational data from physical buildings into the virtual model. Data flows include **sensor configurations** (e.g., environmental, occupancy, and operational metrics) transmitted via **standardized extension points** within a **modular architecture**, ensuring adaptability to evolving sensor deployments. The system supports **bidirectional communication** with **event-driven triggers** (e.g., alarms, threshold breaches) to maintain synchronization between physical assets and their digital counterparts. **Data quality assurance** is implied through the framework’s runtime configuration management, allowing dynamic adjustments without full redeployment. Preprocessing steps are not explicitly detailed, but the framework’s **service-oriented design** enables modular integration of custom analytics and validation pipelines. Communication protocols are abstracted within the Hono/Ditto ecosystem, with no explicit mention of lower-layer specifics (e.g., MQTT, CoAP) in the provided documents.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: The **virtual-to-physical interaction** in this DT framework is characterized by **bidirectional control flows** that feed **control signals** back to Building Control Systems (BCS) for real-time adjustments. The framework supports **runtime configuration management**, enabling dynamic reconfiguration of control logic without redeployment, though explicit mechanisms for **conflict resolution** or **command validation** are not detailed. Control messages are inferred to be abstracted within the framework’s **modular architecture**, with no mention of specific payload formats (e.g., JSON) or authentication protocols (e.g., OAuth 2.0). Feedback systems are implied through the bidirectional nature of the framework, ensuring closed-loop operation between the DT and physical systems. Specific control targets (e.g., HVAC, security) are not mentioned beyond the general reference to **lighting systems** in related research contexts.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection:
The **technical network infrastructure** for this DT framework is designed to support **real-time data exchange** via **Eclipse Hono** and **Ditto**, which serve as the core enablers for bidirectional communication. Key technical details include:
- **Communication protocols**: Primarily **Eclipse Hono** (a telemetry ingestion framework) and **Ditto** (a lightweight IoT data management platform), though specific lower-layer protocols (e.g., MQTT, OPC UA) are not mentioned. The framework leverages these tools to abstract protocol-specific details.
- **Network topology and architecture**: The document does not specify a detailed network topology (e.g., edge/core layers, SDN), but the **modular architecture** implies a **service-oriented design** with standardized extension points for heterogeneous systems.
- **Bandwidth and latency requirements**: Not explicitly quantified, but the emphasis on **real-time synchronization** suggests low-latency requirements, likely optimized through the chosen tools (Eclipse Hono/Ditto).
- **Reliability and redundancy mechanisms**: Addressed indirectly via the framework’s **scalability** and **maintainability** requirements (NF_X), implying built-in fault tolerance and high availability, though specifics (e.g., replication, failover) are not detailed.
- **Security protocols**: The **interoperability** and **accessibility** NF_X requirements imply robust security measures, though explicit protocols (e.g., TLS, VPN) or encryption standards are not mentioned.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment:
The **deployment infrastructure** for this DT framework is characterized by a **model-driven approach** leveraging **Eclipse Hono**, **Ditto**, and **Unreal Engine** for visualization and interaction. Key deployment aspects include:
- **Hosting platforms**: The framework does not specify traditional cloud/edge/on-premise deployments but instead relies on **Unreal Engine** for immersive visualization and **Eclipse Hono/Ditto** for data management, implying a **hybrid or application-specific deployment** model.
- **Computational and storage requirements**: Not explicitly detailed, but the framework’s **scalability** (NF_X) suggests adaptive resource allocation, likely optimized for **low-overhead updates** and **real-time processing**.
- **Scalability mechanisms**: The **modular architecture** and **runtime configuration management** enable horizontal scaling of components without full redeployment, supporting growth from individual components to entire infrastructure systems.
- **Deployment models supported**: The framework is tailored for **AECO (Architecture, Engineering, Construction, Operations) domain** challenges, emphasizing **BIM-to-DT integration** and **operational life cycle management**, rather than generic cloud/edge models.
- **Availability/reliability assurance**: Addressed through **maintainability** (NF_X) and **low-overhead updates**, ensuring continuous operation with minimal downtime, though explicit SLAs or redundancy strategies are not provided.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages:
The Digital Twin (DT) system described in the provided documents supports a **comprehensive life cycle** spanning **device modeling, deployment, runtime synchronization, and custom service integration**, aligned with the limitations of Building Information Modeling (BIM) as a static representation framework. The lifecycle is structured to address the gap between BIM’s static nature and the dynamic operational requirements of DTs.

1. **Device Modeling Phase**:
   - **Representation Type**: Enriched BIM models extended with **Building Control System (BCS) components**, including sensors, actuators, and controllers.
   - **Transition Management**: Model-Driven Engineering (MDE) principles are applied to transform BIM models into structured, deployable DT artifacts with **minimal manual intervention**.
   - **Governance**: Building Control Engineers define control logic and initiate DT deployment from BIM, ensuring semantic alignment between physical and digital representations.

2. **DT Deployment Phase**:
   - **Representation Type**: Automatically generated **deployable DT artifacts** via model-to-model and model-to-text transformations (implied by MDE principles).
   - **Transition Management**: Framework leverages **standardized extension points** to integrate custom services and plugins, enabling modular evolution.
   - **Governance**: DT Engineers develop and integrate custom services, while Facility Managers interact with the twin for operational oversight.

3. **Runtime Synchronization Phase**:
   - **Representation Type**: **Real-time data exchange** between physical infrastructure and the DT, ensuring continuous synchronization.
   - **Transition Management**: Immersive **interactive visualization** (e.g., Unreal Engine) provides interfaces for monitoring and control, bridging the gap between static BIM and dynamic DT operations.
   - **Governance**: Facility Managers monitor system states and operate physical infrastructure via the DT, with runtime analytics supporting adaptive decision-making.

4. **Custom Service Integration Phase**:
   - **Representation Type**: **Modular DT architecture** with standardized interfaces for integrating custom analytics, plugins, and services.
   - **Transition Management**: Evolution of DT functionality is supported through **plugin-based extensions**, allowing adaptation to changing operational needs.
   - **Governance**: DT Engineers extend functionality via custom services, ensuring scalability and adaptability of the DT system.

The lifecycle governance approach emphasizes **stakeholder-specific roles** (Building Control Engineers, Facility Managers, DT Engineers) and **modular, extensible architectures** to accommodate dynamic operational requirements while mitigating BIM’s limitations.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution:
The twinning process is **model-driven**, leveraging MDE principles to automate the transition from BIM-based device models to deployable DT artifacts. Building Control Engineers initiate deployment by defining BCS components within BIM, which are then **semantically enriched** to support subsequent transformations. The framework employs **automated deployment pipelines** to generate runtime-ready DT components, reducing manual intervention and ensuring reproducibility.

DT evolution is managed through a **modular architecture** with standardized extension points, enabling DT Engineers to integrate custom services and plugins. Versioning is implicitly handled via the framework’s **modular design**, where new services are deployed as independent modules without altering core DT infrastructure. Quality assurance is embedded in the **MDE workflow**, where semantic consistency is validated during model transformation phases. Testing is not explicitly detailed in the documents, but the framework’s **automated generation** implies validation through runtime synchronization checks and stakeholder feedback loops (e.g., Facility Manager interactions). No explicit version control system (e.g., Git-based) or semantic validation protocols (e.g., SPARQL queries) are mentioned.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: The framework provides **real-time monitoring and simulation capabilities** to support decision-making, enabling Facility Managers to visualize system states and operational metrics via **immersive interfaces** (e.g., Unreal Engine). Insights are derived from **continuous data exchange** between physical and digital assets, though specific analytics (e.g., predictive maintenance, energy optimization) are not detailed. Decision-making processes rely on **interactive visualization** for manual adjustments, with no explicit mention of automated analytics (e.g., machine learning) or confidence measures. User interaction is facilitated through **Facility Manager-driven operations**, where manual overrides and state monitoring occur via the DT interface. No technical details on confidence metrics (e.g., Bayesian inference, uncertainty quantification) or alert types (e.g., threshold-based anomalies) are provided.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The framework adheres to **Building Information Modeling (BIM)** standards as its foundational data representation layer, though specific schema standards (e.g., IFC) are not mentioned. Model-Driven Engineering (MDE) principles govern the transformation pipeline, ensuring structured artifact generation. The runtime environment integrates **Eclipse Hono and Ditto**, which provide standardized protocols for device connectivity and data exchange, though no explicit adherence to protocols like MQTT, CoAP, or OPC UA is stated. No compliance with industry standards (e.g., ISO/IEC 42010, IEC 62433) or certification frameworks is referenced. Interoperability is implied through the framework’s **modular architecture**, enabling third-party service integration via standardized extension points, but no formal interoperability standards (e.g., OMG MDA) are cited.
extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations: The provided documents highlight critical security and safety considerations in Digital Twin (DT) systems, particularly within the AECO (Architecture, Engineering, Construction, and Operations) domain. While explicit protocols like cybersecurity frameworks or access control mechanisms are not detailed, the documents emphasize **semantic coherence** and **runtime adaptability** as foundational requirements for maintaining system integrity. Abbasi et al. [49] specifically address **semantic drift**, a phenomenon where misalignment between digital and physical models introduces risks to system reliability, necessitating **synchronization techniques** to mitigate divergence. Kamburjan et al. [50] propose **declarative life cycle specification languages** to enforce dynamic evolution and rule-based management of DT instances, indirectly implying **formal governance mechanisms** to ensure compliance and reduce operational risks. Additionally, the reliance on **proprietary ecosystems** (e.g., Bentley’s iTwin.js [32]) underscores the need for **access control mechanisms** to manage stakeholder permissions and prevent unauthorized modifications. However, no explicit mention of authentication systems (e.g., OAuth, role-based access control), threat mitigation strategies (e.g., encryption protocols, intrusion detection), or fail-safe mechanisms (e.g., redundancy protocols, failover systems) is provided in the context of AECO-specific DT implementations. The documents also do not elaborate on compliance with data protection regulations (e.g., GDPR, ISO/IEC 27001) or consent management frameworks, leaving these aspects unaddressed in the surveyed works.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
_•_ **R2:** An automated deployment process that reduces manual intervention and enables reproducible DT setups ( **C2** ).

_•_ **R3:** Real-time data integration using Eclipse Hono and
Ditto, enabling live synchronization between physical
assets and digital twins ( **C3** ).

_•_ **R4:** A modular architecture with defined extension points
to support scalable, service-oriented DT development in
AECO ( **C4** ).

Together, these contributions aim to operationalize scalable
and interoperable DTs in AECO contexts. By closing the gap
between design-time models and runtime operations, we offer
a sustainable path toward domain-wide DT adoption.
Our work is organized using Design Science Research
(DSR) [59] and provides artifacts to facilitate model-driven deployment of operational control DTs in the AECO domain. We
start with requirements elicitation (cf. Section III-A) and conclude with prototyping and experimentation (cf. Section V).



Our artifacts are developed in response to the following design
science problem [59]:


**Improve** _DT_ _deployment_ _in_ _BIM-based_ _infrastructure_
_projects_ (context)
**by designing** _a model-driven automation framework_ (artifact)
**that satisfies** _semantic integration, real-time connectivity, and_
_extensibility_ (requirement)
**in order to** _enable scalable DT engineering in the AECO_
_domain_ . (goal)


III. BIM- BASED D IGITAL T WINS

BIM is increasingly regarded as the principal modelling
paradigm in the AECO sector, providing a comprehensive
framework for generating, managing, and disseminating digital
building information throughout the entire building life cycle
with policy initiatives and regulatory mandates decisively
fostering and accelerating this development [60]. BIM enables
the creation of semantically rich, object-based 3D models that
incorporate geometric, spatial, and functional characteristics
of building components and their relationships. These models provide a collaborative platform where architects, engineers, and construction professionals can coordinate design
decisions, detect clashes, simulate performance, and generate
documentation [61].
While there are also DTs and industry-specific solutions
for the digital mapping of assets in other industries, such
as the manufacturing industry (e.g. capitalizing on Modelbased Systems Engineering; MBSE) [1], [62], BIM is becoming the standard solution in the construction industry [63],

block_1_judge.reasoning: The extraction is highly specific and fully grounded in the source text. It accurately describes the AECO domain, BIM-based nature, and the specific characteristics of the models (e.g., 'semantically rich, object-based 3D models'). It correctly identifies the collaborative framework and lifecycle focus. Quotes supporting this include: 'BIM enables the creation of semantically rich, object-based 3D models that incorporate geometric, spatial, and functional characteristics' and 'These models provide a collaborative platform where architects, engineers, and construction professionals can coordinate design decisions, detect clashes, simulate performance'.

The extraction provides a highly specific and detailed list of services that align perfectly with the paper's contributions and abstract. It correctly identifies Real-time Monitoring, Model-Driven Automation, Optimization and Control, and Visualization and Simulation as key services. The capabilities, such as live synchronization and automated deployment, are directly supported by the text. Target users are also consistent with the 'Actor Roles' table and general context. Supported by quotes: 'enabling real-time monitoring, simulation, and life cycle-encompassing optimization', 'An automated deployment process that reduces manual intervention and enables reproducible DT setups', and 'leveraging Eclipse Hono and Ditto for real-time data exchange'.

The characteristic correctly identifies key tools like Eclipse Hono, Eclipse Ditto, BIM, and the Model-Driven Automation Framework, describing their roles accurately based on the text. It also correctly identifies Design Science Research (DSR) as the methodological framework. However, it misses 'Unreal Engine', which is explicitly mentioned in the Abstract and Section I as a key component for 'rich, interactive visualization'. The descriptions for the included tools are well-supported. Supported by quotes: 'integrates Eclipse Hono and Ditto for real-time data exchange', 'model-driven framework for scalable DT deployment', and 'Our work is organized using Design Science Research (DSR)'.

block_2_docs_retrieved:
Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime



configuration management further enhances adaptability by
enabling adjustments without requiring full redeployment.
Complementing these functional requirements, our nonfunctional requirements ( **NF** _X_ ) address critical aspects of
interoperability across heterogeneous systems, scalability from
individual components to entire infrastructure systems, maintainability through low-overhead updates, and accessibility for
users with varying technical expertise.
This requirements-driven approach provides a systematic
foundation for implementing DTs that bridge the gap between
BIM’s static representations and the dynamic operational
needs of the AECO domain, addressing the challenges identified in Section II. At this, we want to emphasize that these
are not all requirements, but rather those pertinent to crafting a
technological spike solution tailored to the specific challenges
and innovations explored in this work (cf. Section V-C). The
discussion section will revisit these requirements to evaluate
their effectiveness and role in advancing the proposed solution.


_B. Solution Proposal_


Figure 1 (p. 6) presents the conceptual model of our
framework, illustrating how the requirements from Table III
are implemented through coordinated roles and system components. Figure 1 demonstrates the flow from enriched BIM
models to operational DTs, emphasizing the key roles and their
contributions to the DT life cycle.
_Building Control Engineers_, using BIM tools, perform
device modeling and initiate the deployment process. _Facility_
_Managers_ then interact with the deployed DT for real-time
monitoring and control. _DT Engineers_ extend the framework’s
capabilities through service integration. The DT Framework
acts as a central hub, facilitating bidirectional data exchange
and enabling a holistic approach to building life cycle management.


IV. M ODEL  - BASED D EPLOYMENT OF D IGITAL T WINS

TABLE I: Actor Roles in our Model-driven DT Deployment
Framework


**Role** **Description**


Building Control Engineer Models and configures devices, defines the control logic, and initiates
DT deployment from BIM models.
Facility Manager Monitors system states, interacts
with the twin, and operates the
physical infrastructure via the DT.
DT Engineer Develops and integrates custom services and plugins to extend DT
functionality.


Commensurate with the limitations of BIM as a seamless

enabler for DTs, we have developed a comprehensive set of
requirements for our model-driven deployment framework.
These requirements emerge from both theoretical considerations and practical insights gained through our research
projects, TwinLight and BOREALIS (cf. Section II).
To address the gap between BIM’s static representations and
the dynamic nature of DTs, our proposal must support the
complete life cycle of DT deployment and operation. Table II
outlines the use cases to be supported by our framework,
spanning from initial device modeling to the development
of custom DT services atop our proposal. These use cases
reflect the core functionality needed by different stakeholders
(cf. Table I) involved in the DT life cycle, including _Building_
_Control Engineers_ who model devices and implement control
logic, _Facility Managers_ who monitor and control building operations, and _DT Engineers_ who extend functionality through
custom services.

The functional requirements ( **F** _X_ ) detailed in Table III form
the conceptual foundation of our solution, beginning with
device modeling capabilities that address BIM’s fundamental
limitation in representing BCS components. This enables
building control engineers to define sensors, actuators, and
controllers directly within the BIM environment, creating a
unified semantic foundation for subsequent transformation
steps. The framework then leverages MDE principles to extract
structured representations from these enriched BIM models
and automatically generate deployable DT artifacts with minimal manual intervention.

Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime

Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime



configuration management further enhances adaptability by
enabling adjustments without requiring full redeployment.
Complementing these functional requirements, our nonfunctional requirements ( **NF** _X_ ) address critical aspects of
interoperability across heterogeneous systems, scalability from
individual components to entire infrastructure systems, maintainability through low-overhead updates, and accessibility for
users with varying technical expertise.
This requirements-driven approach provides a systematic
foundation for implementing DTs that bridge the gap between
BIM’s static representations and the dynamic operational
needs of the AECO domain, addressing the challenges identified in Section II. At this, we want to emphasize that these
are not all requirements, but rather those pertinent to crafting a
technological spike solution tailored to the specific challenges
and innovations explored in this work (cf. Section V-C). The
discussion section will revisit these requirements to evaluate
their effectiveness and role in advancing the proposed solution.


_B. Solution Proposal_


Figure 1 (p. 6) presents the conceptual model of our
framework, illustrating how the requirements from Table III
are implemented through coordinated roles and system components. Figure 1 demonstrates the flow from enriched BIM
models to operational DTs, emphasizing the key roles and their
contributions to the DT life cycle.
_Building Control Engineers_, using BIM tools, perform
device modeling and initiate the deployment process. _Facility_
_Managers_ then interact with the deployed DT for real-time
monitoring and control. _DT Engineers_ extend the framework’s
capabilities through service integration. The DT Framework
acts as a central hub, facilitating bidirectional data exchange
and enabling a holistic approach to building life cycle management.

block_6_docs_retrieved:
[11]. Most frameworks are not designed with AECO-specific
standards like BIM in mind, and those that can be adapted
— such as Bentley’s iTwin.js [32] — rely on proprietary
ecosystems or require substantial manual effort due to a lack
of automation.

Recently, Gerber et al. [33] presented an ad-hoc DT for
a Living Lab for a LEED Platinum academic building based
on BIM for monitoring, simulation, and performance analysis.
Their study emphasizes automated data acquisition, normalization, and 3D visualization, while highlighting challenges
in data integration and stakeholder coordination, insights that
directly inform model-driven approaches in DT engineering.
Their results reinforce earlier findings by Opoku et al. [34]
who also demonstrated the effective integration of DTs with
BIM and IoT platforms to optimize indoor environmental conditions in a university library setting. Both studies underscore
the significance of real-time data utilization and visualization
in enhancing facility management and highlight common challenges in data integration and stakeholder engagement in DT
implementations. In synopsis, AECO adoption is dominated by
commercial tools or custom-built solutions [2], [13]. Notably,
MDE is not present across the surveyed works, leaving a
critical gap in abstraction and reuse capabilities essential for
complex domains like AECO.
Model-driven engineering (MDE) offers a streamlined approach to handling the increasing complexity of systems by
elevating models to primary engineering artifacts [35], [36].
It enables abstraction, formalization, and automation across
the development life cycle, reducing cognitive overload and
improving consistency in large-scale systems [37]. MDE supports the definition of domain-specific modeling languages
and metamodels that unify engineering workflows, providing
a structured foundation for communication across disciplines
and life cycle phases [38], [39]. Importantly, MDE also
strengthens governance by encoding organizational policies
and architectural decisions into formal models, promoting
compliance, reuse, and quality assurance [40]. Recent advances continue to refine the integration of MDE into DT
systems [41]–[47]. As reported in Lehner et al.’s recent survey,
research primarily focuses on runtime adaptability, semantic
coherence, and tool interoperability [48]. Abbasi et al. [49]
examined the phenomenon of _semantic drift_, where the alignment between digital models and their physical counterparts
diverges over time. Their study stresses the necessity of
synchronization techniques to ensure the integrity of modelbased DTs. Kamburjan et al. [50] proposed a declarative life
cycle specification language, enabling dynamic evolution and
rule-based management of DT instances. Meanwhile, Oakes et
al. [51] advanced ontological frameworks to support service
